summary: Swedish king (r. 1611-1632) of the House of Vasa, nicknamed the "Lion of the North," whose intervention turned the tide of the Thirty Years' War for the Protestant cause. Famous for defeating the Count of Tilly at the Battle of Breitenfeld (1631) and for dying without armor at the Battle of Lutzen (1632). Also won the Battle of the Lech (Rain), where Tilly was mortally wounded, and ended a long war with Poland via the Treaty of Altmark (1629). Modernized the Swedish army with the allotment conscription system and the light Hakkapeliitta cavalry. Father of the future Queen Christina.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Gustavus Adolphus is identified in giveaways as the "Lion of the North," the Protestant King of Sweden who led his country's House of Vasa into the Thirty Years' War. He also founded the city of Gothenburg, and relied heavily on his chancellor Axel Oxenstierna, who directed Sweden's war effort after the king's death.

clues.clue: Nicknamed the "Lion of the North" for his military success in the Thirty Years' War
examples: name this king of Sweden who was known as the 'Lion of the North' for his military success in the 30 Years War.
For 10 points, name this protestant King of Sweden during the Thirty Years War known as the 'Lion of the North.'

clues.clue: Protestant King of Sweden of the House of Vasa who led his country into the Thirty Years' War, intervening on behalf of the Lutherans
examples: Name this 'Lion of the North,' a Swedish king of the Vasa dynasty who intervened on behalf of the Lutherans during the Thirty Years' War.
Gustavus Adolphus led this country into the Thirty Years' War while head of its royal house of Vasa.

clues.clue: Relied on his chancellor Axel Oxenstierna, who continued directing the war effort after the king's death
examples: After this victor at the Battle of Rain was killed at the Battle of Lutzen, his chancellor Axel Oxenstierna continued to fight in his stead.
this man who was advised by Axel Oxenstierna defeated Count von Tilly at the Battle of Breitenfeld

clues.clue: Founded the city of Gothenburg
examples: This founder of Gothenburg had earlier been victorious at the Battle of Breitenfeld but died in victory against Wallenstein during the Battle of Leutzen.

works.name: Battle of Breitenfeld (1631)
works.description: Gustavus Adolphus's decisive 1631 victory over Imperial forces under the Count of Tilly near Leipzig, in which more mobile Swedish troops outflanked Tilly's traditionally arranged tercios after the Saxon allies were routed. The victory is often paired with his introduction of the allotment conscription system and his personal command of the Hakkapeliitta cavalry.

clues.clue: Defeated the Count of Tilly's forces at the (First) Battle of Breitenfeld, with Swedish troops outflanking Tilly's traditionally arranged tercios
examples: This man's troops outflanked those of the Count of Tilly in his win at the Battle of Breitenfeld.
Breitenfeld was a victory for this Swedish king, who introduced the allotment system and entered Sweden into the Thirty Years' War before his death at Lutzen.

clues.clue: Personally led the Hakkapeliitta cavalry into the fighting at Breitenfeld
examples: This 'Lion of the North' personally led the Hakkapeliitta at the Battle of Breitenfeld.

works.name: Battle of Lutzen (1632)
works.description: Gustavus Adolphus was killed at the Battle of Lutzen in 1632, a year after Breitenfeld, while fighting the Imperial cavalry of Albrecht von Wallenstein. He reportedly entered the battle without armor, declaring the Lord God would be his protection, and was fatally shot after being dragged by his horse. His death led to a rift between Wallenstein and Emperor Ferdinand II.

clues.clue: Killed at the Battle of Lutzen (1632), a year after his Breitenfeld victory, while fighting the Imperial cavalry of Wallenstein
examples: died in victory against Wallenstein during the Battle of Leutzen.
This king's death came after he entered the 1632 Battle of Lutzen with no protection, claiming 'the Lord God is my armor!'

clues.clue: Entered the battle without armor, declaring "Let the Lord God be my armor!"; was fatally shot after being dragged by his horse
examples: This king reportedly entered battle without armor, bravely stating 'Let Lord God be my armor!'; this intelligent plan failed following a battle where he was dragged by his horse and shot in the head while down.
This king's death came after he entered the 1632 Battle of Lutzen with no protection, claiming 'the Lord God is my armor!'

clues.clue: Following his death, Wallenstein was charged with treason for refusing to attack the now-leaderless Swedish army
examples: Wallenstein was charged for treason due to his refusal to attack the Swedes after this king's death.

works.name: Battle of the Lech / Rain (1632)
works.description: Earlier in 1632, Gustavus Adolphus crossed the Lech River to defeat Imperial forces at the Battle of Rain, mortally wounding the Count of Tilly. The campaign's high point came after his earlier defense of Stralsund against Imperial siege.

clues.clue: Was victorious after constructing a pontoon bridge over the Lech River
examples: In one battle, this leader was victorious after constructing a pontoon bridge over the Lech River.

clues.clue: Sent the Hakkapeliitta cavalry across the Lech at the Battle of Rain, where his nimble light artillery fatally wounded the Count of Tilly
examples: This leader employed a corps of lightly armoured horsemen named for their battle cry, the Hakkapeliitta, which they sent across the river Lech at the Battle of Rain at which the Count of Tilly was killed.
his nimble light artillery fatally wounded the Count of Tilly.

clues.clue: This campaign's high water mark came after his earlier defense of Stralsund against Imperial siege
examples: The defense of Stralsund began this man's most storied campaign, which reached its high water mark at the Battle of the Lech.

works.name: Polish War & the Treaty of Altmark
works.description: Before intervening in the Thirty Years' War, Gustavus Adolphus fought a long war against Sigismund III of Poland, defeating General Koniecpolski and gaining control of Livonia; the conflict ended with the Truce (Peace) of Altmark in 1629, freeing him to invade Germany.

clues.clue: Gained control of Livonia by signing the Treaty (Truce) of Altmark with Poland (1629), ending his war against Sigismund III
examples: This leader gained control of Livonia after signing the Treaty of Altmark with Poland.
This man defeated General Koniecpolski in a war that ended with the Peace of Altmark.

clues.clue: Suffered a defeat at the Battle of Trzciana during this Polish campaign, where his experimental leather cannons were captured
examples: This leader attempted to increase battlefield manoeuvrability through the development of leather cannon, several of which were captured in his defeat at the battle of Trzciana.

works.name: Early Wars: the Kalmar and Ingrian Conflicts
works.description: Early in his reign, Gustavus Adolphus fought two smaller wars against Sweden's neighbors: the Kalmar War against Denmark, which he nearly lost, and the Ingrian War against Russia, which he won.

clues.clue: Gained the fortress of Noteborg via the Treaty of Stolbovo, ending the Ingrian War
examples: This ruler gained control of the fortress of Noteborg by the Treaty of Stolbovo, which ended the Ingrian War.

clues.clue: Nearly drowned at the Battle of Vittsjo during the Kalmar War, and after losing was forced to pay a large indemnity at the Peace of Knared
examples: This ruler nearly drowned at the Battle of Vittsjo, and after losing one war he was forced to pay a substantial indemnity at the Peace of Knared.

works.name: Military and Administrative Reforms
works.description: Gustavus Adolphus modernized the Swedish state and army: he introduced the allotment conscription system and new parish regulations to ease taxation, funded his wars with revenue from the Falun Copper Mine, fielded the light Hakkapeliitta cavalry, experimented with lightweight leather cannons, and adopted linear tactics later cited as the start of the "Military Revolution."

clues.clue: Introduced the allotment (indelning) system of conscripting soldiers by district
examples: Breitenfeld was a victory for this Swedish king, who introduced the allotment system and entered Sweden into the Thirty Years' War before his death at Lutzen.

clues.clue: Instituted new parish regulations, making it easier to tax and conscript his subjects
examples: This ruler instituted the new parish regulations, making it easier for him to tax and conscript.

clues.clue: Boosted his treasury with revenue from the Falun Copper Mine
examples: His treasury was boosted by the Falun Copper Mine.

clues.clue: Deployed the Hakkapeliitta, lightly armored cavalry named for their battle cry
examples: This leader employed a corps of lightly armoured horsemen named for their battle cry, the Hakkapeliitta.
The most famous utilizer of the Hakkapeliita, for 10 points, name this Swedish loser of the Battle of Lutzen.

clues.clue: Experimented with lightweight leather cannons; after they failed, lightened his artillery carriages and placed them under Lennart Torstensson's command
examples: This leader attempted to increase battlefield manoeuvrability through the development of leather cannon.
After his failure to create leather cannons, he lightened his artillery carriages, which he would put under Torstenson's command.

clues.clue: Reintroduced linear tactics and shock cavalry, developments later cited by historian Michael Roberts as the start of the "Military Revolution"
examples: Roberts's theory of the Military Revolution emphasized the return to linear tactics and use of shock cavalry implemented by this king from the Vasa dynasty.

clues.clue: Promoted the Scotsman Alexander Leslie to Field Marshal; his army included the Scottish Mackay's Regiment and General Johan Baner
examples: This man promoted Scotsman Alexander Leslie to Field Marshal, and his army included the Scottish Mackay's Regiment and General Johan Baner.

works.name: Family and Legacy
works.description: Gustavus Adolphus's family and afterlife supplied recurring clues: his wife Maria Eleonora's severe postpartum depression, his daughter and successor Christina, and his continued commemoration in Sweden.

clues.clue: His wife Maria Eleonora suffered severe postpartum depression, reportedly attempting to drop their newborn daughter down a flight of stairs, and after the king's death forced that daughter to sleep beneath a casket containing his heart
examples: In a historical case of post-partum depression, this ruler's wife often attempted to drop their newborn daughter down a flight of stairs, and following the death of her husband, forced that daughter to sleep under a casket containing his heart.

clues.clue: His daughter Christina succeeded him as monarch upon his death
examples: Christina was the daughter of this Swedish ruler who was killed at the Battle of Lutzen while fighting in the Thirty Years War.

clues.clue: Honored in Sweden each year on November 6 (Gustavus Adolphus Day) with celebrations including pastries topped with chocolate or marzipan reliefs of him
examples: This military leader's legacy is honored in his home country each year on November 6 in celebrations including pastries with chocolate or marzipan reliefs of this man.

comprehensive_summary: Gustavus Adolphus (Gustav II Adolf, 1594-1632), of the House of Vasa, ruled Sweden from 1611 until his death. Early in his reign he fought two smaller wars against Sweden's neighbors: the Kalmar War against Denmark, in which he nearly drowned at the Battle of Vittsjo and was forced to pay a large indemnity at the Peace of Knared after losing, and the Ingrian War against Russia, which he won, gaining the fortress of Noteborg via the Treaty of Stolbovo. He then fought a long war against Sigismund III of Poland, defeating General Koniecpolski and gaining Livonia, though he suffered a defeat at the Battle of Trzciana where his experimental leather cannons were captured; that war ended with the Truce of Altmark in 1629. At home, he modernized the Swedish state, introducing the allotment conscription system and new parish regulations that eased taxation, boosting his treasury with revenue from the Falun Copper Mine, promoting the Scotsman Alexander Leslie to Field Marshal, and fielding an army that included the Scottish Mackay's Regiment and General Johan Baner. His military reforms — lightweight leather cannons (later abandoned in favor of lightened artillery carriages under Lennart Torstensson), the light Hakkapeliitta cavalry, and a return to linear tactics and shock cavalry — were later cited by historian Michael Roberts as the start of the "Military Revolution." Freed by the Truce of Altmark, Gustavus Adolphus led Sweden into the Thirty Years' War on behalf of the Lutherans, though his failure to relieve the besieged city of Magdeburg led to its horrific sack. He won his signature victory over the Count of Tilly at the (First) Battle of Breitenfeld (1631), where more mobile Swedish troops, personally led into action alongside the Hakkapeliitta, outflanked Tilly's traditionally arranged forces after the allied Saxons were routed; the victory is often linked with his introduction of the allotment system. Early the following year he crossed the Lech River — after constructing a pontoon bridge and sending the Hakkapeliitta across — to win the Battle of Rain, where his nimble light artillery mortally wounded the Count of Tilly; that campaign's high point followed his earlier defense of Stralsund. Gustavus Adolphus was killed at the Battle of Lutzen later in 1632, a year after Breitenfeld, while fighting the Imperial cavalry of Albrecht von Wallenstein; he had reportedly entered the battle without armor, declaring "Let the Lord God be my armor!", and was fatally shot after being dragged by his horse. His death led Wallenstein to be later charged with treason for refusing to attack the now-leaderless Swedish army, while his chancellor Axel Oxenstierna took over direction of Sweden's war effort. Nicknamed the "Lion of the North" and remembered as the founder of Gothenburg, Gustavus Adolphus was married to Maria Eleonora, who suffered severe postpartum depression — reportedly attempting to drop their newborn daughter down a flight of stairs, and after the king's death forcing that daughter to sleep beneath a casket containing his heart. That daughter, Christina, succeeded him as monarch. He remains honored in Sweden each year on November 6 (Gustavus Adolphus Day) with celebrations including pastries topped with chocolate or marzipan reliefs of him.
recursive_suggestions: Gustav I Vasa — appeared as its own answerline (T8, Chicago Open 2012); founder of the Vasa dynasty and an ancestor of Gustavus Adolphus, with his own clues (Dalecarlian revolt, Treaty of Malmo, exile of Gustav Trolle, Henry Brooke play banned in 1737) — do not merge with Gustavus Adolphus

Thirty Years' War — appeared as its own answerline (T1) with Literature-flavored clues (Mother Courage and Her Children, Simplicius Simplicissimus, Tears of the Fatherland); broader topic than Gustavus Adolphus's involvement
Queen Christina — his daughter and successor, recurring standalone answerline (abdication, conversion to Catholicism, occupancy of the Riario Palace, attempt on the Polish throne)
Albrecht von Wallenstein — recurring standalone answerline as the Imperial general blamed for/who killed him at Lutzen
Count of Tilly (Johann Tserclaes) — recurring standalone answerline as his Catholic opponent at Breitenfeld and the Lech, and leader of the sack of Magdeburg
Magdeburg — recurring standalone answerline for the 1631 sack, tangential to Gustavus Adolphus's failed relief of the siege
Battle of Nordlingen (1634) — recurring standalone answerline for the Swedish/Protestant defeat two years after his death; watch for confusion since it postdates him
